currency.name: Euro
currency.budgetNote: Recommended: 30 EUR (Croatia switched from kuna to euros in 2023)

mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Croatia on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe, in the south. Croatia has a long curved Adriatic coastline, with Italy across the sea to the west 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Croatia?
mapQuestions.answer: Zagreb, in the north, inland. About 800,000 people. Mostly visited by travellers as a stop on the way south to the coast.

mapQuestions.question: What language does Croatia speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Croatian, a Slavic language written with the Latin alphabet. Most younger Croats and people working in tourism also speak excellent English.

mapQuestions.question: How many islands does Croatia have?
mapQuestions.answer: Over 1,200 islands and islets along the Dalmatian coast. Only about 50 are inhabited. Most are limestone hills sticking out of the bright blue Adriatic.

mapQuestions.question: When did Croatia become its own country?
mapQuestions.answer: 1991, when it declared independence from Yugoslavia. Croatia joined the EU in 2013 and adopted the Euro in 2023.

foods.name: Grilled fish
foods.description: Whole Adriatic fish (sea bream, sea bass, mackerel) brushed with olive oil, grilled simply, served with chard and potato. The Dalmatian coast classic.

foods.name: Crni rižot
foods.description: Black risotto made with cuttlefish and its squid ink, giving a deep black colour. Coastal Dalmatian specialty.

foods.name: Peka
foods.description: Lamb, octopus or veal slow-cooked under a heavy iron bell ("peka") that is buried in coals for hours. Tender, smoky, traditional. Order ahead.

foods.name: Pag cheese
foods.description: A hard, salty sheep's cheese from the windswept island of Pag, where the sheep eat aromatic herbs blown across from the sea. World-class.

foods.name: Burek
foods.description: Flaky pastry filled with meat, cheese or spinach, sold by the slice at every bakery. A Balkan tradition shared across the region.

foods.name: Sladoled
foods.description: Croatian ice-cream, especially good in Zagreb. Italian-style gelato traditions with Croatian local flavours like wild blackberry and pistachio.

scavengerItems.label: A clear-blue Adriatic cove with pebble beach
A walled medieval town (Dubrovnik's walls are 25 m tall and you can walk around the entire city)
Diocletian's Palace in Split (a Roman emperor's palace, still lived in 1,700 years later)
A waterfall at Plitvice or Krka National Park
A sailboat moored in a tiny island harbour
Fishermen unloading the day's catch at a small Dalmatian port
A "klapa" group singing traditional Dalmatian a cappella songs on a square
A ferry between islands (Hvar, Vis, Brač, Korčula)
The Pula Roman amphitheatre (one of the best-preserved Roman amphitheatres outside Rome)
A pine marten or wild dolphin off Lošinj (the Adriatic dolphin sanctuary)

geoMatches.description: The capital. Upper Town with cobbled streets and a Gothic cathedral, Lower Town with grand 19th-century squares and museums (the Museum of Broken Relationships is a hit).
The "Pearl of the Adriatic". A walled stone city on the southern coast, used as King's Landing in Game of Thrones. Walk the 2 km of walls around the entire city.
A coastal city built INSIDE the walls of a 1,700-year-old Roman palace (Diocletian's). People still live and run shops in the original Roman walls.
A national park of 16 turquoise lakes connected by hundreds of waterfalls and wooden walkways. UNESCO World Heritage. Most photographed place in Croatia.
A long lavender-scented island off the Dalmatian coast, with old-stone villages, vineyards and clear coves. The "sunniest island in Croatia".
The triangular peninsula in the north-west. Hilltop medieval towns, truffle forests, olive groves and the Roman amphitheatre at Pula.

senses.smell: Pine trees on Adriatic cliffs, olive oil on hot bread, fresh fish on a grill, lavender drying in island fields, sea-salt spray on a sailboat…
senses.hear: "Bok!" and "Hvala!" everywhere, Adriatic waves slapping pebbles, "klapa" a cappella singing in a stone square, church bells across an Old Town, the wind ("bura") whistling between island peaks…
senses.taste: Salty grilled fish with squeezed lemon, sharp Pag cheese, sweet ripe figs straight off a tree, smoky lamb peka, lemony Croatian olive oil…
senses.feel: The smooth cold Adriatic on a hot day, sun-warmed limestone walls, the bumpy walk on a pebble beach, the breeze on a sail, the polish of a 1,700-year-old Roman flagstone in Split…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why does Split live inside a Roman palace?
stories.body: Around the year 305 AD, the Roman emperor Diocletian retired and built himself a giant fortified seaside palace at what is now Split, in modern Croatia. After Diocletian died, the palace was abandoned. About 300 years later, refugees from a sacked nearby Roman town moved INTO the empty palace, divided the imperial halls into homes, and never left. Today, 1,700 years after Diocletian's death, around 3,000 people still live inside the original palace walls. The basement halls are restaurants, the old temples are churches, the imperial mausoleum is now the cathedral, and the courtyard hosts opera concerts. Split is one of the only places on Earth where you can sleep in a Roman palace and order morning coffee inside it.

stories.question: Why are the lakes so turquoise?
stories.body: Plitvice Lakes National Park is 16 connected lakes that step down a forested valley, joined by hundreds of waterfalls. The water is a vivid blue-green not because of dye but because of dissolved minerals (especially calcium carbonate). The minerals deposit on rocks, moss and fallen wood, slowly building up natural travertine dams that create the lakes. The colour shifts during the year, from emerald in winter to brilliant turquoise in summer. You can't swim in the lakes (to protect the delicate travertine), but you can walk on a wooden path that loops past dozens of waterfalls and over crystal water. UNESCO put Plitvice on the World Heritage list in 1979.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A whole lake landscape is built and re-shaped by minerals depositing on logs and rocks. What other slow, invisible processes shape places we love?

stories.question: Why is Dubrovnik a Game of Thrones star?
stories.body: Dubrovnik's old town is one of the best-preserved medieval walled cities in Europe: 2 km of stone walls up to 25 m tall, surrounding a tight maze of marble streets, baroque churches and red-tiled rooftops. Game of Thrones used it extensively as "King's Landing", capital of the Seven Kingdoms, from season 2 onward. Suddenly Dubrovnik was on every fantasy fan's travel list. Walking the city walls (about 90 minutes) takes you past most of the locations seen on the show. Dubrovnik was so successful that other Croatian filming locations (Trsteno arboretum for the King's Landing gardens, Klis fortress for Meereen) became smaller Game of Thrones destinations too.

stories.question: Why is there a giant Roman amphitheatre in Istria?
stories.body: In the small Istrian town of Pula on the north Croatian coast sits a 2,000-year-old Roman amphitheatre. The "Pula Arena" was built between 27 BC and 68 AD, and originally seated 23,000 spectators for gladiator games. It is one of the SIX biggest Roman amphitheatres still standing anywhere (the Colosseum is the biggest, then Capua, then El Jem in Tunisia, then Verona, then Pula). Today the Pula Arena hosts summer film festivals and concerts (think: thousands of people sitting on 2,000-year-old stone benches watching modern films projected on a screen). Visiting in summer feels like time travel.

stories.question: What is "klapa" singing?
stories.body: Walk through a Dalmatian stone square on a summer evening and you might hear five or six men standing in a tight circle, no instruments, singing rich harmonies that fill the space. This is "klapa", a Croatian Dalmatian a cappella tradition. The songs are mostly about the sea, fishing, love and longing for home, with one lead voice and several harmony parts. UNESCO put klapa on its "intangible cultural heritage" list in 2012. Klapa is sung on every Dalmatian island. Stop and listen.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Croatia so far?
Old town, beach, island, waterfall, which has felt the most "Croatia"?
How does the Adriatic compare to any other sea you have swum in?
Did you stop and listen to a klapa group? What did it sound like?
Split has been a living city inside a Roman palace for 1,700 years. What would that feel like to grow up in?

wordSearch.hint: Croatian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Brown bear
animals.description: About 1,000 brown bears live in Croatia, mostly in the Gorski Kotar forests. Hard to see in the wild.

animals.name: Grey wolf
animals.description: Returned to Croatian forests after near-extinction. A few hundred live in the mountains.

animals.name: Bottlenose dolphin
animals.description: In the Adriatic around Lošinj, the only resident dolphin population in the Croatian Adriatic. Protected.

animals.name: Mediterranean butterflies
animals.description: Hundreds of species across the Croatian coast and islands, especially in the limestone hills above Dubrovnik.

animals.name: Kotor cat
animals.description: Cats roam the cobbled streets and city walls of Croatian Adriatic towns, especially Hvar and Dubrovnik. Practically national mascots.

animals.name: Loggerhead sea turtle
animals.description: Rare sightings in the southern Croatian Adriatic, mostly around Mljet and the Lastovo archipelago.

animals.name: Griffon vulture
animals.description: A huge vulture with a 2.8 m wingspan, nesting on the cliffs of Cres island in the northern Adriatic.
